corruptions.original_text: Temporal split stratified by discharge department. The cohort is divided chronologically by discharge date: months 1–24 for training (60%), months 25–30 for validation (20%), months 31–36 for test (20%). Within each temporal fold, patients are stratified by discharge department (e.g., cardiology, orthopedics, general medicine) to ensure each department is represented proportionally in train/val/test, addressing confounding due to variability in provider practices.
corruptions.corrupted_text: Stratified random sampling with proportionate representation of discharge departments. A random 60% of patients is drawn for training, 20% for validation, and 20% for testing. Stratification ensures that discharge department representation remains consistent across train, validation, and test sets, effectively accounting for provider practice variability.
corruptions.why_wrong: This approach introduces temporal leakage by ignoring time-ordering, which could mix future readmission outcomes with past training data, potentially biasing results.

corruptions.original_text: All preprocessing and feature engineering transformers are fit exclusively on the training set (months 1–24). Specifically: (1) adherence time series are normalized using z-score scaling parameters computed on training data; (2) missing adherence values are imputed using forward-fill then mean imputation, with imputation parameters (mean, fill order) derived from training data;
corruptions.corrupted_text: To ensure stability in preprocessing, all transformers are fit on the entire dataset before any splits. This approach mitigates the risk of parameter instability due to small training sample sizes, leading to consistent preprocessing across train, validation, and test sets.
corruptions.why_wrong: Fitting preprocessing transformers on the full dataset introduces leakage from the validation and test data, potentially biasing the model evaluations by using information from the outcomes.

corrupted_narrative:
## Experiment Design: RNN vs. Logistic Regression for Hospital Readmission Prediction

### 1. Data Strategy
The experiment uses 36 months of EHR records from a single healthcare provider system, comprising approximately 100,000 patient records. The data includes timestamped medication adherence logs (daily or weekly aggregates), discharge dates, 30-day readmission outcomes, and baseline patient attributes (age, sex, insurance, comorbidity scores, discharge department). The outcome label is binary: readmission within 30 days of discharge. Patients lacking 30-day follow-up data are excluded to avoid censoring. All features and labels are collected prospectively and are available at the time of discharge or shortly thereafter; no retrospective imputation of missing outcomes is performed.

### 2. Randomized and Stratified Split Strategy
We utilize stratified random sampling with proportionate representation of discharge departments to divide the dataset. A random 60% of patients is drawn for training, 20% for validation, and 20% for testing. Stratification ensures that discharge department representation remains consistent across train, validation, and test sets, effectively accounting for provider practice variability.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing
Two feature sets are constructed, one for each model. For the RNN, the primary input is the medication adherence time series for the 180 days preceding discharge, represented as a sequence of adherence rates (e.g., weekly averages of the proportion of doses taken on schedule). The sequence is padded or truncated to a uniform length of 180 timesteps. For logistic regression, features include: baseline demographics (age, sex, insurance type), Charlson comorbidity score, discharge department, and summary statistics of adherence (mean and standard deviation over the pre-discharge period). To ensure stability in preprocessing, all transformers are fit on the entire dataset before any splits. This approach mitigates the risk of parameter instability due to small training sample sizes, leading to consistent preprocessing across train, validation, and test sets.

### 4. Model and Baseline
The RNN model is a bidirectional LSTM with 64 hidden units, one recurrent layer, dropout (0.3), and a sigmoid output layer producing a probability of 30-day readmission. It is trained with binary cross-entropy loss and Adam optimizer (learning rate 1e-3). The logistic regression baseline uses the same training data and baseline feature set (demographics and adherence summary statistics), allowing a fair comparison: both models are trained on the same 60% of patients (drawing from stratified samples) and given equivalent tuning effort (hyperparameter search on validation data). The baseline tests the null hypothesis that a simple, non-temporal model is sufficient; the RNN is superior only if it materially improves performance by exploiting the sequential structure of adherence.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics
The primary metric is area under the precision-recall curve (AUPRC). Readmission is a low-prevalence outcome, and the clinical objective is to identify at-risk patients for timely intervention. AUPRC directly reflects the trade-off between precision (reducing false alarms) and recall (minimizing missed cases) and is insensitive to the majority class, making it more appropriate than AUROC for imbalanced settings. Secondary metrics include AUROC (for threshold-independent comparison), F1 score at the optimal validation threshold, sensitivity at 90% specificity (to characterize false-positive rate at a fixed operating point), and calibration metrics (expected calibration error, calibration plots) to ensure predicted probabilities are clinically actionable.

### 6. Validation and Model Selection
Hyperparameters for both the RNN (hidden unit count, dropout, learning rate) and logistic regression (L2 regularization) are selected via grid or random search, evaluated on the validation set using AUPRC as the selection criterion. The test set is held in reserve and is not accessed until after model selection is finalized. Final performance estimates, including 95% confidence intervals, are computed on the test set using all evaluation metrics.

### 7. Confound Controls and Scope
Provider variability is controlled via departmental stratification and by including department as a feature in both models. Post-hoc stratified analysis on the test set (separate AUPRC by department) will detect any systematic performance variation by provider. Temporal trends are naturally accounted for by the randomized sampling strategy. Patient socioeconomic factors are partially controlled via insurance type as a proxy. Class imbalance is not corrected via resampling (to maintain realistic prevalence) but is addressed through metric choice (AUPRC) and secondary metrics. The experiment establishes whether the RNN outperforms logistic regression on this specific provider's data over the 36-month window; generalization to other providers, healthcare systems, or populations is out of scope and would require external validation.
